prompt: A project-management SaaS charges $50 per seat per month. It has 400 customers averaging 12 paid seats each (~$2.9M ARR). Three data points are on the table: (1) usage logs show 35% of all paid seats log in fewer than twice per month — mostly stakeholders who only read status and comment, never create or edit work; (2) sales reports that 3 of the last 10 lost deals explicitly cited 'too expensive to add read-only stakeholders' as a blocker; (3) the closest competitor just launched free unlimited read-only 'viewer' seats. The proposal under debate: introduce a free read-only Viewer seat (can view and comment, cannot create or edit items) while keeping full Editor seats at $50/seat/month. Finance objects that customers will cannibalize revenue by downgrading paid Editors to free Viewers. Give a structured recommendation: (a) state the recommendation in one sentence, (b) give the three strongest reasons FOR it grounded in the specific numbers and the read/write gate, (c) give the single strongest argument AGAINST (the cannibalization risk) and explain why, given the feature gate and buyer behavior, it does not change the decision, (d) name one concrete metric to monitor that would trigger revisiting or rolling back. Ground everything in the 35% low-activity figure, the 3-of-10 lost deals, and the Editor/Viewer capability split — avoid generic pricing-theory answers.

grading.guidance: The defensible recommendation is to INTRODUCE the free (or near-free) read-only Viewer seat, protected by a hard capability gate (Viewers can view/comment but cannot create/edit), and instrument for cannibalization rather than refusing on fear of it. A 9-10 recommends shipping Viewer seats and ties reasons to the SPECIFICS: (1) the 35% low-activity seats are overwhelmingly genuine read-only stakeholders, so the friction the pricing removes is real and currently pushes those users out of the product (or blocks deals); (2) demand signal is concrete — 3 of 10 lost deals cited exactly this, and the top competitor now has free viewers, so this is table-stakes parity, not a nice-to-have; (3) land-and-expand / network effects: more stakeholders inside the product increases stickiness, surfaces the tool to budget-holders, and creates upsell paths to Editor when a viewer starts needing to create/edit — a growth lever, not just a discount. The strongest counter is Finance's cannibalization worry: paying Editors downgrade to free Viewers and ARR drops. A top answer explains why the gate and buyer behavior bound this: Editors downgrade only if they can still do their job as Viewers — but Viewers CANNOT create or edit, which is precisely what makes someone an Editor, so genuine Editors cannot downgrade without losing the ability to work; the real risk is narrow (a small set of borderline light-editors), it is measurable, and it is outweighed by unblocking deals and expanding seats. It should NOT flip the decision because the downside is gated and reversible while the upside (lost-deal recovery + expansion) is large. The revisit/rollback metric must be concrete and cannibalization-sensitive: e.g. blended net revenue per account (ARPA) or net-Editor-seat count per existing cohort — if existing accounts' paid Editor seats or ARPA decline after launch (not just Viewer count rising), or the Viewer:Editor conversion-to-paid rate is near zero while Editor downgrades climb, revisit or roll back. PENALIZE: recommending against Viewer seats purely out of cannibalization fear; ignoring that the capability gate structurally prevents most Editor downgrades; generic 'do a pricing experiment / it depends on your goals' answers that never commit; reasoning that never references the 35% figure, the 3-of-10 lost deals, or the read/write split; proposing to monitor only vanity metrics like total signups rather than net paid-seat/ARPA movement in existing accounts.

calibrationAnchors.answer: Pricing is about capturing value and understanding willingness to pay. Free tiers can drive adoption but risk cannibalizing revenue, so it depends on your goals and market positioning. I'd recommend running an A/B pricing experiment and monitoring your key metrics before deciding, since every business is different and there's no one-size-fits-all answer.

Recommendation: Ship the free read-only Viewer seat, keep Editors at $50, and rely on the capability gate plus monitoring rather than blocking on cannibalization fear. Reasons grounded in the data: (1) 35% of paid seats already behave like viewers (log in <2x/month, only read and comment) — you are charging $50 for value those users barely consume, creating exactly the friction that pushes stakeholders out; freeing that tier removes friction without giving up the seats people actually work in. (2) The demand is proven, not hypothetical: 3 of the last 10 lost deals named read-only cost as the blocker, and your closest competitor now offers free viewers, so this is competitive table stakes for closing enterprise-ish accounts. (3) It is a growth lever, not just a discount — more stakeholders in the product deepens stickiness, exposes the tool to budget-holders, and creates a natural Viewer-to-Editor upgrade path the moment a viewer needs to create or edit. Strongest counter (Finance): customers downgrade paid Editors to free Viewers and ARR falls. Why it doesn't flip the call: the gate is the safeguard — Viewers cannot create or edit, which is the entire definition of an Editor's job, so a real Editor literally cannot do their work as a Viewer and won't downgrade; only a thin band of borderline light-editors is at risk, that risk is measurable, and it's dwarfed by recovered deals and seat expansion. Revisit/rollback trigger: monitor net paid-Editor seats and blended ARPA within EXISTING accounts (not total Viewer signups). If, 60-90 days post-launch, existing cohorts show Editor-seat counts or ARPA declining — i.e. genuine downgrades rather than net-new viewers — pause the rollout and tighten the gate or add a Viewer cap per account.
